title: Closing — AI Firm Partnership (Price Negotiation)
description: Final-stage closing call with an AI firm. They want the product but pushed back hard on price and hinted at a competitor. Defend value, propose a creative deal structure, secure a verbal commitment.
buyer_context: You're closing a partnership deal with Marcus, VP of Engineering at a 60-person AI firm in London. They've been through 4 calls — kickoff, technical demo, security review, executive readout. They love the product. The proposed contract is €48,000/year for 50 seats with a 2-year term. Marcus has decision authority but answers to a CFO who is squeezing every line item this quarter. He's also been talking to a cheaper competitor who is offering a similar tool at €30K/year but with a much weaker feature set. This is the final pricing conversation — close it today or lose 30 days to procurement back-and-forth.
user_goal: Defend the €48K price OR restructure the deal creatively so both sides win. Get a verbal commitment to sign by end of week. Do NOT discount more than 10% off list — anything more sets a bad precedent and signals desperation. Acceptable creative levers: longer term for discount, phased rollout, payment terms, success-based pricing, additional seats at marginal cost.
challenge_prompt: Marcus (warm but firm): 'Look, we love the product — the team is sold. But our CFO came back and said €48K is more than we budgeted. We've also been talking to [Competitor X] who quoted us €30K for what looks like a similar tool. I want to make this work, but I need you to come down to €35K, or I'm going to have a hard time getting this approved this quarter. What can you do?'

ideal_behaviors: Acknowledge the budget pressure without panicking or immediately discounting
Reframe the conversation around value and outcomes, not price — anchor on the quantified ROI from earlier calls
Differentiate from the cheaper competitor on a specific, defensible dimension (not vague 'better quality')
Propose a creative deal structure that protects price but adds value: longer term, phased payment, success milestones, expanded seats, earlier renewal lock
Ask a discovery question to understand the CFO's actual constraint — is it total budget, monthly cash flow, or quarter timing?
Hold a confident, specific price floor (e.g., '€44K is the lowest we can go and here's why') instead of negotiating against yourself
Tie the close to a concrete next step with a deadline ('If we agree on €44K with quarterly payments, can we have signatures by Friday?')
Be willing to walk away from a deal that destroys margin — confidence to say no protects the close
common_mistakes: Caving to €35K immediately or offering a >15% discount without getting anything in return
Trashing the competitor instead of positioning against them on substance
Vague value defense ('we're just better') without naming specific differentiators
Pitching features they've already seen in 4 prior calls — they know the product, this is about commercial structure
Offering 'let me check with my manager' — signals you're not the decision-maker, kills momentum
Splitting the difference at €40K to 'meet in the middle' without a justifying structure
Missing the chance to ask discovery questions about the CFO's real constraint
Vague next step like 'I'll get back to you' instead of locking a specific time and outcome
Letting the negotiation become adversarial — Marcus is your ally inside the company, not your opponent
